nativeRange.habitat: Mixed coral and rubble areas of lagoon and seaward reefs, from the lower surge zone down to roughly 40-45 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical coral reef with abundant live rock, branching corals, and cover, typically encountered as solitary individuals.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on filamentous algae and small invertebrates. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of marine flakes and pellets, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, chopped meaty seafoods, and dried marine algae or seaweed sheets.

avoidWith: newly added tankmates after the damsel is established

summary: The Ocellate Damselfish is a small Indo-Pacific species with a blue body that fades to yellow or orange toward the tail and a distinctive black spot (ocellus) at the rear of the dorsal fin. Generally peaceful as a juvenile, it tends to become territorial with age but is broadly regarded as reef-safe and one of the more manageable marine fish for beginners.
careNotes:
The Ocellate Damselfish is widely considered one of the hardier and more beginner-accessible marine fish, though its temperament shifts noticeably as it matures. Juveniles tend to be relatively peaceful, but adults commonly become semi-aggressive and territorial, particularly toward other damselfish or similarly sized fish. Keeping a single specimen is generally advisable in a modest reef setup; aggression tends to be more manageable in larger tanks with plenty of broken sightlines and hiding spots.

Adults reach roughly 7.5-10 cm in length. A minimum tank of around 150 liters is a reasonable starting point, though more space is beneficial once the fish is fully grown, especially when keeping it alongside other semi-aggressive species. Provide abundant live rock with caves and branching coral for shelter, along with open mid-water swimming space; moderate to strong flow is appreciated.

Feeding is generally straightforward. The species accepts a varied diet of marine flakes and pellets, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, chopped meaty seafoods, and dried marine algae or seaweed sheets. Wild populations show several color forms across their geographic range, so coloration can vary somewhat between specimens.
breedingNotes: The Ocellate Damselfish is oviparous, forming pairs during the breeding period. Eggs are demersal — meaning they are laid and adhere directly to the substrate — and males have been reported to guard and aerate the clutch until hatching. Captive breeding is not commonly documented for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care level Easy, temperament Aggressive (matures into semi-aggressive/territorial), reef-safe, water parameters (pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, 72-78 F), 30 US gal minimum, max 3.5 in, omnivore, native range stated as Fiji. Browsed via product page reached from /collections/damselfish.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pomacentridae), max length 10.0 cm FL, depth 0-45 m, Pacific Ocean distribution (Moluccas to Samoa, Izu Islands to Rowley Shoals and New Caledonia), water temperature 25.4-28.9 C, omnivorous (algae + small invertebrates), oviparous with male egg-guarding, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: AquariumDomain (Princess Damselfish profile)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~3 in), temperament (peaceful as juvenile, semi-aggressive with age), reef-safe, 30 gal minimum but better with more room, water parameters (72-80 F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025), prefers moderate to strong flow with abundant live rock.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs form during breeding and spawn demersal eggs that adhere to the substrate. Males have been reported to guard and aerate the clutch until hatching. Larval rearing is not commonly documented for hobbyists.
